transcript:
Right now you're watching quick little cyan-white streaks dart across a dark field, and off in the corners, slow orange bruises of light are quietly swelling. It feels like two different things happening at once — and that's exactly the point.

Here's the trick: in the code, there's an array called "influence written" — a grid the departures silently mark as they fly past. Each streak adds to a cell's count in "write influence at," but the streak itself never checks what it caused. Only when a cell crosses "threshold for arrival" does an orange glow get born — by which point the particle that triggered it is long gone, off-screen or dead. Watch how the halo's growth is keyed to "growth frames," a slow square-root expansion — deliberately much slower than any single particle's lifespan, so the cause and its consequence can never share a frame.

So what you're seeing isn't cause and effect side by side — it's effect, alone, after its cause has already left the building. The white streaks aren't fading into orange. They're just gone, and the orange is what's left where they used to be.
